articleSummary: This article critically examines popular JavaScript Promise documentation and libraries, arguing that many widely cited resources perpetuate harmful anti-patterns—most notably the `deferred` pattern—which add unnecessary complexity without improving upon traditional callbacks. Analyzing examples from CallbackHell.com, StrongLoop, RisingStack, and the Q library, the author adopts a constructive, code-review tone to guide developers toward cleaner, more functional asynchronous patterns. Framed around the "callback mountain" metaphor and structured as a practical warning guide, the piece targets JavaScript developers seeking to modernize their async code and avoid legacy pitfalls. Readers are directed to a companion GitHub repository for concrete best practices and functional alternatives.
